lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market credit unions providing recurring deposit, payment, consumer-lending, mortgage, member-service, and related financial services to eligible external members.
lens.excluded_activities: Commercial banks, savings institutions, nondepository lenders, corporate credit unions serving other credit unions, credit-union service organizations classified elsewhere, captive finance units, and shells without operating member relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Members place insured share deposits and use loans, payments, and account services; the credit union earns interest spread and fees to cover operating costs, losses, and required net worth, while residual economic value belongs to members through retained capital, dividends, rates, fees, and services.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for broad NAICS 522 credit intermediation, not task hours for credit unions: office and administrative support accounts for 39.76% of employment, business and financial operations 26.16%, and management 11.70%, with large teller, customer-service, loan-clerk, and new-account roles. I map document intake, verification, servicing, member communications, reconciliation, fraud triage, compliance drafting, and routine analysis to high AI exposure; relationship judgment, final credit decisions, cash custody, exception handling, governance, and accountable approvals to partial or low exposure. The broader industry includes securities and nondepository activities that may overstate analytical and sales exposure, while smaller credit unions' less-automated workflows may make the proxy understate remaining opportunity.
a.rationale: Credit unions combine a large clerical and financial-processing workforce with rule-bound records and recurring member interactions, but current core systems already automate many transactions and regulated decisions still require human review and accountability.
a.change_evidence: Credit-union call-center and back-office time studies, wage-weighted by role and separating already-automated work from manual intake, servicing, fraud, compliance, lending, and member support, would materially change the range.
caveats: The OEWS source covers broad NAICS 522 rather than exact-industry credit unions and measures occupations rather than tasks.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated by core banking, rules engines, online account servicing, and robotic process automation.
Fair-lending, privacy, model-risk, cybersecurity, and explainability obligations preserve accountable human work around high-impact decisions.

rho.bridge: NCUA says credit unions are increasingly exploring AI for member services, fraud detection, risk assessment, and operational efficiency and requires board oversight, controls, monitoring, and vendor due diligence. Treasury reports increasing financial-sector AI use alongside privacy, bias, consumer-harm, cybersecurity, and third-party risks. I translate sector use cases and supervisory expectations into five-year implementation of the exposed credit-union workflow, discounting for small-institution budgets, legacy cores, limited technical staff, vendor concentration, data sensitivity, validation, and change management. This may overstate implementation where core-provider integration binds and understate it where shared vendors distribute compliant tools at scale.
rho.rationale: Member-service, fraud, document, and internal-knowledge workflows have credible vendor-supported paths, but regulated decisioning and small-credit-union resource constraints make adoption slower and more review-intensive than technical capability alone suggests.
rho.change_evidence: An NCUA or credit-union league survey reporting production AI use by workflow and asset size, implementation cost, core-provider integration, hours avoided, review time, incidents, and model-validation outcomes would materially change the estimate.
caveats: NCUA describes exploration, use cases, and expectations but does not report a representative production-adoption rate.
Treasury evidence spans the financial sector and may reflect institutions with much greater resources than lower-middle-market credit unions.
Third-party adoption can accelerate access while concentrating operational, privacy, and model risk.

e.rationale: Credit unions fit the recurring external-service lens operationally, but they are not-for-profit, member-owned, member-controlled cooperatives that do not issue stock. An outside financial buyer therefore cannot ordinarily purchase voting equity or take control as it could in a stock company. The small eligible share allows for unusual charter conversions or structures that produce a qualifying control change without treating ordinary vendor contracts or non-control service arrangements as ownership.
e.change_evidence: A legal and transaction-level census of credit unions in the supplied economic band identifying charter form, permissible acquirers, conversion eligibility, member-vote outcomes, transaction consideration, and whether outside investors obtained control would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The supplied firm count applies an EBITDA margin to a cooperative depository model for which EBITDA is not a natural size measure and may materially misstate the screened population.
A merger into another credit union transfers operations but does not create ordinary seller equity value for an outside sponsor.
State-chartered credit-union conversion rules can differ from federal rules.

s5.bridge: NCUA reports that federally insured credit unions fell from 4,455 at year-end 2024 to 4,287 at year-end 2025, a 3.8% net annual decline consistent with long-running consolidation. I convert that one-year net decline to a moderated five-year probability for an eligible institution, recognizing that repeated annual attrition would cumulate to roughly the high teens. Net institution decline is not completed qualifying transfers: it can include liquidations and conversions and is reduced by new charters, so it may overstate merger probability; conversely, merger activity that does not reduce the insured count one-for-one could make it understate activity.
s5.rationale: Credit-union consolidation is persistent and member-approved mergers are an established path, but the relevant event is a qualifying control transfer rather than mere charter disappearance, so the range sits below a mechanical five-year extrapolation of recent net attrition.
s5.change_evidence: NCUA merger-event files linked to each institution's assets, operating status, member vote, consideration, continuing charter, charter conversion, liquidation status, and transaction effective date over ten years would materially change the range.
caveats: The source reports net count decline, not a cohort-level probability of qualifying control transfer.
A credit-union merger is governed by member ownership and regulatory approval rather than conventional seller succession.
Recent consolidation may be concentrated among institutions smaller than the supplied economic band.

q.bridge: NCUA states that credit-union economic value is owned by members and that retained earnings must balance net-worth needs with dividends, lower loan rates, other immediate member returns, and future member services. I translate that cooperative surplus-allocation rule into the discounted share of an implemented gross benefit retained within the operating institution over five years. This may understate retention when capital requirements, growth, or technology reinvestment absorb savings, and overstate it when member expectations and rate competition pass savings through quickly.
q.rationale: Savings can initially support capital and reinvestment, but the absence of outside stockholders and the member-benefit mandate sharply limit durable private capture compared with a shareholder-owned bank.
q.change_evidence: Panel data for credit unions adopting specific automation tools, linking verified cost savings to subsequent net-interest margins, fee changes, deposit and loan pricing, member dividends, technology spending, and retained capital, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The source explains surplus disposition but does not measure the retention of technology savings.
Capital needs and supervisory expectations vary materially with growth, asset risk, and institution condition.
Benefits may appear as better member pricing or services rather than operator cash flow.

d5.bridge: NCUA reports that federally insured credit-union membership rose by 2.4 million to 144.7 million in 2025, loans grew 4.6%, and assets grew 5.4%; the Federal Reserve reports that 94% of adults were banked in 2024. I translate member growth and broad account persistence into a modest five-year increase in constant-quality credit-union service quantity, discounting nominal asset and loan growth for inflation, interest-rate effects, balance-size changes, and consolidation. Aggregate growth may overstate the typical smaller institution because NCUA separately reports falling membership at many small credit unions, while new fields of membership and digital reach could raise growth.
d5.rationale: Households continue to need insured transaction, savings, and credit services, and aggregate credit-union membership is growing, but mature account penetration, consolidation, fintech competition, and weak membership trends at smaller institutions limit the base case.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of credit-union-level inflation-adjusted transaction counts, active-member cohorts, loan originations, deposit accounts, payment volumes, and service usage by asset size and channel would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Asset and loan balances are nominal financial stocks, not constant-quality service quantities.
Aggregate membership growth is concentrated and may not represent institutions in the supplied economic band.
Commercial banks, fintechs, and nonbank payment providers can take activity even when household use of financial services remains durable.

o.rationale: Mobile self-service, automated underwriting, embedded finance, and nonbank payment tools can remove branch and staff interactions, but deposit insurance, custody, lending, fraud responsibility, regulatory reporting, and member governance still require an accountable chartered operator. The discount recognizes substitution toward commercial banks and fintech-fronted products and the possibility that smaller credit unions merge away even when their services continue elsewhere.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal household and business transaction data that attribute deposits, payments, and credit to credit unions, commercial banks, fintech-fronted charter partners, and software-only alternatives, paired with channel-level labor and accountability mapping, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Digital delivery can eliminate interactions without eliminating the regulated institution behind the service.
The operator-required share for credit unions specifically is lower than for depository institutions collectively because customers can switch charter types.
No source directly measures year-five software-only substitution for the exact current service basket.

narrative.executive_view: Credit unions contain substantial document, servicing, fraud, compliance, and member-support work that AI can assist, but their member-owned cooperative form is a fundamental barrier to conventional outside control and private capture. Persistent consolidation does not erase the distinction between a member-approved credit-union merger and an equity acquisition.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can classify and extract documents, draft member communications, support fraud and anomaly detection, summarize cases, assist servicing, search policy, prepare compliance work, and prioritize exceptions. Humans and boards remain accountable for lending, fair treatment, model governance, cybersecurity, complaints, suspicious activity, exceptions, and member governance.
narrative.value_capture: Operational savings can fund capital, resilience, technology, and service expansion, but credit-union economic value belongs to members and is ultimately expressed through rates, fees, dividends, services, or retained net worth. Durable private capture by an outside owner is therefore structurally limited.
narrative.firm_availability: Federally insured credit-union counts continue to decline through consolidation, yet most credit unions issue no stock and are controlled democratically by members. Ordinary mergers can transfer operations to another cooperative; only unusual conversions or legally permissible structures resemble an outside control opportunity.
narrative.demand_durability: Insured accounts, payments, and credit remain durable household needs, and aggregate credit-union membership and loans are growing. Smaller institutions face weaker member growth and channel substitution, so industry demand does not guarantee survival of a particular charter.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are the unsuitable EBITDA-based firm-count anchor, non-stock ownership, member and regulator approvals, core-vendor dependence, model bias and explainability, privacy, cyber fraud, capital requirements, interest-rate exposure, and the gap between aggregate system growth and smaller-credit-union performance.
narrative.principal_driver: Rule-bound financial processing and recurring member service create a sizable workflow-automation opportunity.
narrative.principal_weakness: Member-owned non-stock governance prevents ordinary outside ownership and directs economic value back to members rather than financial sponsors.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 522130 Credit Unions
sources.supports: Exact-industry scope: cooperatives accepting members' share deposits and offering consumer loans to members.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 522000
sources.supports: Broad credit-intermediation occupation mix, including office and administrative support, financial operations, tellers, customer service, and loan clerks.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI)
sources.supports: Credit-union AI use cases, operational opportunities, board oversight, risk controls, data security, monitoring, and third-party due diligence.

sources.title: Treasury Releases Report on the Uses, Opportunities, and Risks of Artificial Intelligence in Financial Services
sources.supports: Increasing financial-sector AI use and material privacy, bias, consumer-harm, cybersecurity, compliance, and third-party risks.

sources.title: NCUA Releases Fourth Quarter 2025 Credit Union System Performance Data
sources.supports: Federally insured credit-union membership, asset and loan growth, institution counts, charter mix, and long-running consolidation.

sources.title: Bank to Credit Union Conversions
sources.supports: Member-owned, member-controlled, not-for-profit cooperative structure, lack of stock, elected boards, field-of-membership limits, and conversion and merger requirements.

sources.title: Evaluating Earnings
sources.supports: Credit-union surplus belongs to members and must balance retained net worth with dividends, lower rates, services, growth, and future member benefits.

sources.title: Report on the Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households in 2024: Banking and Credit
sources.supports: Persistence of bank and credit-union account ownership, credit access, overdraft use, and nonbank financial-service substitution.
